projects.title: how do we see the world

gallery.caption: Opening screen — a question posed to the viewer before anything loads.
Navigation built entirely from hyperlinks, each pointing somewhere unexpected.
Typography responds to scroll position, collapsing as the reader moves through.
Color palette shifts between sections, anchored by a fixed monochrome grid.
Final spread — the links loop back to the beginning.
Cover — letterpress texture simulated through layered CSS filters.
Exposed type specimens treated like photographic negatives.
Red safelight mode toggled by clicking anywhere on the spread.
Detail of grain — noise applied per-character at varying densities.
Back matter: a darkroom timer counts down in the margin.
Signal interference visualized as overlapping sine waves in SVG.
Each track is a function — amplitude and frequency editable in realtime.
Waveform canvas rendered frame-by-frame with requestAnimationFrame.
Mixing board UI — sliders built in pure CSS with custom properties.
Output state: all signals zeroed, leaving a flat line across the screen.
Homepage — a thermostat dial rendered in CSS, interactive on hover.
Room view: each device listed as a typographic element, not an icon.
Schedule interface using a 24-column grid mapped to hours of the day.
Night mode — the entire palette shifts via a single CSS variable swap.
Print spread companion: the same interface translated to physical form.
Source material: scanned fragments arranged before digitization.
Drag-and-drop canvas — elements snap to a hidden 12-column grid.
Layer panel: z-index visualized as a physical stack of paper.
Export view — the collage flattens into a single composited image.
Detail: torn-edge masks drawn in SVG clipPath per element.
Opening argument — Roman vs. Italic set side by side at 72pt.
Historical specimens from Aldus Manutius, annotated on hover.
CSS font-style: oblique demonstrated at 0°–30° with a live slider.
Variable font axis mapped to scroll depth — the type tilts as you read.
Final page: a single italic question mark, centered, unresolved.
